figures.caption: Figure 3: Overview of the Automated Simulation Framework. An LLM-as-a-judge first evaluates the semantic coherence of the initial ASR hypothesis. Upon detecting an error, the User Simulator generates a spoken correction instruction via an LLM and TTS. The Interactive ASR then processes this feedback to reason and update the hypothesis, forming an automated correction process.
Figure 4: Comparison of 𝑆2𝐸𝑅 (top) and CER/WER/MER (bottom) reduction trends across three datasets.

Figure 1: Traditional vs. Interactive ASR paradigms. (Top) Traditional systems struggle with named entities (e.g., ”Night” vs. ”Knight”); (Bottom), our proposed Interactive ASR can take user’s spoken language instructions (e.g., ”starts with a K”) as the feedback to update and correct ASR results.
